quick_facts.label: Conducting authority
quick_facts.value: The Ministry of Education entrusts CBSE, Delhi with conducting the Central Teacher Eligibility Test (CTET).

quick_facts.label: Teaching stages covered
quick_facts.value: CTET Paper I is for candidates intending to teach Classes I–V; Paper II is for Classes VI–VIII. Candidates intending both levels must take both papers.

quick_facts.label: Current September 2026 cycle
quick_facts.value: The September 2026 bulletin scheduled Paper II and Paper I for 6 September 2026, with 5 September available if candidate numbers require it. The published online-application and fee deadline was 10 June 2026; dates are cycle-specific and should be checked on CTET’s official site.

quick_facts.label: Certificate validity
quick_facts.value: The CTET qualifying certificate is stated to be valid for appointment for life for all categories; there is no limit on attempts, and qualified candidates may retake CTET to improve their score.

quick_facts.label: Appointment status
quick_facts.value: Passing CTET is an eligibility condition, not an offer of recruitment or employment. The relevant recruiting or appointing authority verifies final eligibility.

sections.heading: What CTET is for
paragraphs: CTET is the Central Teacher Eligibility Test: a teacher-eligibility examination connected to the minimum qualifications framework for appointment to teach Classes I–VIII under the Right to Education framework. CBSE describes the test as a national benchmark intended to support teacher quality in recruitment, and conducts it on behalf of the Ministry of Education.

A CTET result should be understood as a qualifying credential rather than a teaching job. A candidate must still meet the applicable professional qualifications and the recruiting organisation’s rules, then succeed in that organisation’s separate selection process where applicable.
bullets: Paper I aligns with the primary stage, Classes I–V.
Paper II aligns with the elementary/upper-primary stage, Classes VI–VIII.
For both stages, candidates must appear in both papers.

sections.heading: Eligibility: confirm qualifications against the applicable rules
paragraphs: CTET does not treat merely being allowed to sit the examination as final confirmation of appointment eligibility. CBSE directs candidates to the NCTE’s minimum-qualification regulations as amended from time to time, as well as the recruitment rules of the appropriate government, Kendriya Vidyalaya Sangathan (KVS), or Navodaya Vidyalaya Samiti (NVS), as relevant.

Accordingly, a B.Ed. or D.El.Ed. pathway may be relevant to a prospective teacher’s qualification profile, but the candidate should match their exact academic and teacher-education credentials with the paper/stage sought and the rules of the prospective employer. The recruiting agency or appointing authority makes the final verification. The September 2026 bulletin also records that NCTE-notified relaxation of up to 5% in minimum educational-qualification marks may apply to specified reserved categories.
bullets: Check the current NCTE rules before applying.
Check the recruitment rules of the school system or employer targeted.
Keep evidence of educational and teacher-training qualifications ready for later employer verification.

sections.heading: Exam format and qualifying standard
paragraphs: For the September 2026 cycle, CTET uses multiple-choice questions with four options, one mark per question, and no negative marking. Each paper is 2 hours 30 minutes, has 150 questions, and carries 150 marks. The main question paper is bilingual in Hindi and English; candidates select two different languages for Language I and Language II from the listed options.

Paper I contains 30 questions each in Child Development and Pedagogy, Mathematics, Environmental Studies, Language I, and Language II. Paper II contains 30 questions each in Child Development and Pedagogy, Language I, and Language II, plus 60 questions in either Mathematics & Science or Social Studies/Social Science, depending on the teaching subject choice.

The bulletin states that a score of 60% or more is a TET pass. It also says that school managements may consider concessions for SC/ST, OBC, and differently abled candidates under their applicable reservation policies; candidates should therefore verify the particular recruitment notification rather than assume a universal relaxed cut-off.
bullets: Both papers: 150 MCQs, 150 marks, 2 hours 30 minutes, and no negative marking for the September 2026 cycle.

sections.heading: Applying through the official portal
paragraphs: For the September 2026 cycle, CBSE required online application through the CTET or CBSE website. The published workflow was to complete the online form, note the registration/application number, upload a current photograph and signature, pay online, and retain the confirmation page. Candidates were not required to send a hard copy of the confirmation page to the CTET Unit.

Fees and deadlines vary by cycle. In the September 2026 bulletin, the stated examination fees were ₹1,000 for one paper or ₹1,200 for both papers for General/OBC (NCL) candidates, and ₹500 or ₹600 respectively for SC/ST/differently abled candidates, plus applicable bank GST. Those figures and the 10 June 2026 deadline are historical, cycle-specific context—not a fee or deadline for a future cycle.
bullets: Use only the official CTET/CBSE application route.
Read the current information bulletin before submitting the form.
Retain the confirmation page and monitor the official site for admit-card and correction-window notices.

sections.heading: Preparing effectively
paragraphs: Build preparation around the current official information bulletin and its syllabus, not only coaching material. CBSE advises candidates to use authentic textbooks and the syllabus suggested by NCTE. For Paper I, questions are based on NCERT Classes I–V topics, with difficulty and linkages potentially extending to the secondary stage. For Paper II, they are based on NCERT Classes VI–VIII topics, with difficulty and linkages potentially extending to senior-secondary level.

Give substantial study time to pedagogy as well as subject content: Child Development and Pedagogy is compulsory in both papers, and the official syllabus emphasizes diverse learners, inclusive education, learning processes, assessment, and subject-specific pedagogy. Use the official archive’s previous question papers to practise the question style and pacing.
bullets: Select the intended paper(s) first, then map study topics to the official syllabus.
Practise all compulsory components, including both language papers and pedagogy.
Use official past question papers as timed practice; they are not a substitute for checking the current bulletin for syllabus changes.

sections.heading: Where CTET can be used
paragraphs: CTET applies to Central Government schools, including KVS, NVS and Central Tibetan Schools, and to schools under the listed Union Territory administrations. The bulletin says unaided private schools may choose to consider CTET. State-government, local-body, and aided schools ordinarily consider their State TET, although a State Government may consider CTET if it decides not to conduct a State TET.

For CBSE-affiliated schools, the bulletin notes the amended affiliation bye-laws position that teachers appointed from 6 March 2012 to teach Classes I–VIII must qualify CTET or the appropriate Central/State TET. This does not override an employer’s separate academic, professional, subject, vacancy, reservation, interview, document-verification, and recruitment requirements.
Successful candidates receive digital CTET eligibility certificates and all candidates receive digital mark sheets through DigiLocker, according to the September 2026 bulletin.
bullets: Potential pathway: primary teaching roles for Classes I–V after Paper I, subject to employer rules.
Potential pathway: upper-primary teaching roles for Classes VI–VIII after Paper II, subject to employer rules.
CTET can strengthen eligibility for some private-school roles, but private schools may decide whether to consider it.
